---
title: "SHM Worked Example 5.19"
subtitle: "Reference: Applied Mechanics: Reed's Vol. 2"
format:
  pdf:
    pdf-engine: xelatex
    toc: false
    number-sections: false
    geometry: margin=2.5cm
---

**Ex. 5.19**: A helical spring with a mass of 6 kg has a mass of 10 kg attached to its free
end. The mass is pulled down 40 mm from the equilibrium position and then released.

**Given:**

- Mass of the spring, $m_s = 6$ kg
- Mass attached, $m = 10$ kg
- Spring stiffness, $k = 532$ N/m
- Initial displacement (amplitude), $A = 40$ mm $= 0.0400$ m
- Position to find kinetic energy, $x = 30$ mm $= 0.0300$ m

## (i) Frequency of Oscillation

The natural frequency of a mass-spring system is given by:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m_{\text{eff}}}}$$

where the effective mass accounts for the distributed mass of the spring:

$$m_{\text{eff}} = m + \frac{m_s}{3}$$

Substituting the given values:

$$m_{\text{eff}} = 10 + \frac{6}{3} = 12 \ \text{kg}$$

Calculating the angular frequency:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{532}{12}} = \sqrt{44.3333} = 6.6583 \ \text{rad/s}$$

The frequency of oscillation is:

$$f = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{6.6583}{2\pi} = 1.0597 \ \text{Hz}$$

## (ii) Kinetic Energy at $x = 30$ mm

The velocity of the attached mass at displacement $x$ is:

$$v = \omega_n \sqrt{A^2 - x^2} = 6.6583 \times \sqrt{(0.0400)^2 - (0.0300)^2}$$

$$v = 6.6583 \times \sqrt{0.0007} = 6.6583 \times 0.0265 = 0.1762 \ \text{m/s}$$

The kinetic energy of the attached mass is:

$$KE = \frac{1}{2} m v^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times (0.1762)^2 = 0.1552 \ \text{J}$$

## Final Answers

- **Frequency of oscillation:** $f = 1.0597$ Hz
- **Kinetic energy at $x = 30$ mm:** $KE = 0.1552$ J
